The genus Anopheles was established by Johann Wilhelm Meigen in 1818. It is the type genus of the subfamily Anophelinae (Family: Culicidae). Currently, the genus comprises over 460 formally recognized species and numerous species complexes. The designation "spp." is a collective botanical and zoological abbreviation used to refer to multiple species within this genus when individual identification is not specified or required. Taxonomically, the genus is divided into several subgenera, including Anopheles, Cellia, Kerteszia, Lophopodomyia, Nyssorhynchus, and Stethomyia (Meigen, 1818; Harbach, 2013).

Aedes albopictus, commonly known as the Asian tiger mosquito, is one of the most significant invasive vectors of human pathogens globally. It is a highly competent vector for several arboviruses, most notably Dengue (all four serotypes), Chikungunya, and Zika. Unlike Aedes aegypti, which is highly anthropophilic, Ae. albopictus is an opportunistic feeder (eclectic), biting humans, mammals, and birds, which allows it to act as a "bridge vector" for zoonotic diseases such as West Nile virus and various Encephalitis viruses. Furthermore, it is a primary vector for the filarial nematodes Dirofilaria immitis and Dirofilaria repens. Its ecological plasticity, particularly its ability to undergo diapause in the egg stage, has allowed it to colonize temperate regions, significantly expanding the geographical risk area for tropical diseases.
